tracks.label: Product Management

sources.title: Lenny's Newsletter — Shipping AI Features That Stick
Reforge — The AI PM Operating Model
First Round Review — How Media Companies Staff AI Teams

tracks.body:
SAMPLE DATA — replace by running scripts/research.mjs.

1. Ship the boring 80% first
The most-shared AI PM piece this week argues that teams over-index on the flashy generative demo and under-invest in the unglamorous plumbing: eval harnesses, fallback UX, and cost dashboards. Why it matters: a media PM can win trust by shipping a reliable summarizer before a magical one.
Try this: write your eval set before you write the prompt.

2. The "AI feature as a funnel" frame
A widely cited framework reframes every AI feature as a four-stage funnel — trigger, draft, review, accept — and says most drop-off happens at review. Why it matters: instrument the review step and you learn where your model actually loses the user.

3. Hiring note: the rise of the "AI generalist PM"
Several media orgs are folding standalone "AI PM" roles back into core product, expecting every PM to own at least one model-backed surface. Why it matters: position yourself as the PM who makes AI legible to the rest of the org.

4. One tactic for this week
Run a 30-minute "prompt review" with eng and one editor. Read three real outputs aloud. The disagreements you surface are your roadmap.

tracks.label: AI Automations & Claude Craft

sources.title: Claude Docs — Tool use and MCP

tracks.body:
SAMPLE DATA — replace by running scripts/research.mjs.

1. Cite-or-decline prompting
A technique making the rounds: instruct the model to either answer with an inline citation or explicitly say "I can't verify this." It sharply reduces confident-wrong answers in research workflows.
Try this: add 'Every factual claim must carry a source URL or be dropped' to your system prompt.

2. The 'eval as a fixture' pattern
Builders are checking a small JSON eval set into the repo and running it in CI on every prompt change. Cheap, and it catches regressions before users do.
Try this: start with ten input/expected pairs in a tests/ file.

3. MCP for read-only context
People are wiring read-only MCP servers (calendar, docs, tickets) so the agent can pull live context without write access — safer, and easier to get approved at work.
Try this: expose one read-only resource this week and feel the difference.

4. Schedule, don't poll
The cleanest automations run on a cron and commit their output, exactly like this project — no always-on server, fully auditable.

tracks.label: AI Tech World
tracks.body:
SAMPLE DATA — replace by running scripts/research.mjs.

1. New frontier model release (US) — a major lab shipped an updated flagship with a larger context window and lower pricing. Why it matters: cheaper long-context changes what's affordable to automate. [US]

2. EU AI Act enforcement guidance — Brussels published clarifying guidance on high-risk system obligations. Why it matters: compliance timelines just got more concrete for anyone serving EU users. [EU]

3. China open-weight push — a leading Chinese lab released a competitive open-weight model under a permissive license. Why it matters: widens global access and pressures closed providers on price. [China]

4. India compute buildout — new state-backed GPU clusters announced to support domestic startups. Why it matters: shifts where the next wave of AI products gets built. [India]

5. Gulf sovereign AI fund — a Gulf state expanded its AI investment vehicle with fresh capital aimed at infrastructure. Why it matters: capital, not just talent, is going global. [Gulf]

tracks.label: US Political Spectrum
tracks.body:
SAMPLE DATA — replace by running scripts/research.mjs.

STORY 1 — Federal budget negotiations
What actually happened: Congress advanced a stopgap funding measure ahead of the deadline.
• PROGRESSIVE (The Guardian): frames it as a fight to protect social spending from cuts.
• NEUTRAL (AP): reports the vote count, the deadline, and the next procedural step.
• CONSERVATIVE (WSJ): frames it around deficit restraint and fiscal discipline.

STORY 2 — Tech regulation hearing
What actually happened: a Senate committee held a hearing on AI oversight and questioned industry witnesses.
• PROGRESSIVE (Mother Jones): emphasizes consumer protection and corporate accountability.
• NEUTRAL (Reuters): summarizes who testified and what was proposed.
• CONSERVATIVE (National Review): emphasizes innovation costs and regulatory overreach.

STORY 3 — State election administration
What actually happened: a state finalized new rules for the upcoming election cycle.
• PROGRESSIVE (Vox): frames it around ballot access.
• NEUTRAL (AP): lays out the rule changes and effective dates.
• CONSERVATIVE (The Dispatch): frames it around election integrity.
